title: Office - Late to Meeting
question_text: You are 5 minutes late to a meeting. What do you say?
opt1: Sorry I'm late. Traffic was bad.
explanation: Apologize briefly and give a short reason. Don't make excuses or blame others.
example1: Sorry I'm late. Let me catch up. What did I miss?
example2: Sorry everyone. The elevator was stuck.

title: Office - Asking for Vacation
question_text: How do you request time off from your manager?
opt1: Could I request a few days off next month?
explanation: Politely request using 'Could I' or 'May I'. Give advance notice.
example1: Could I take three days off starting the 15th? I have available vacation days.
example2: May I request PTO for the week of Christmas?

title: Office - Computer Problem
question_text: Your computer won't turn on. Who do you call?
explanation: IT support handles technology problems. Most companies have a help desk number or ticket system.
example1: My computer is frozen. Can you send someone from IT to my desk?
example2: I submitted a ticket to IT. They said they'll come within an hour.

title: Office - Answering Phone
question_text: You answer a call at work. What do you say?
opt1: Good morning ABC Company. This is Maria speaking.
explanation: State the company name and your name. It sounds professional and helps the caller know they dialed correctly.
example1: Good afternoon Smith Industries. How may I direct your call?
example2: Thank you for calling. This is John. How can I help you?

title: Factory - Safety First
question_text: You see a coworker not wearing safety goggles. What do you say?
opt1: Hey don't forget your safety goggles. It's the rule.
opt2: I don't care what you do.
explanation: Politely remind them of safety rules. It protects everyone, including you.
example1: The sign says safety glasses required in this area. Let me grab you a pair.
example2: Hey, you need goggles in here. There's a box by the door.

title: Factory - Machine Problem
question_text: A machine starts making a strange noise. What do you do?
opt1: Stop the machine and tell your supervisor.
explanation: Safety first. Stop the machine and report it. Never fix machinery unless you're trained.
example1: The press is making a grinding sound. I shut it down and need maintenance.
example2: That noise doesn't sound right. I'm going to hit the emergency stop.

title: Factory - Heavy Lifting
question_text: A box is too heavy for you to lift alone. What do you say?
opt1: Could you help me lift this? It's too heavy alone.
explanation: Ask for help directly. Lifting alone can cause injury. Team lifting is safer.
example1: This box is over 50 pounds. Can you grab the other side so we can lift together?
example2: Let's lift this together. On three. One, two, three.

title: School - Late to Class
question_text: You arrive 10 minutes late to class. What do you do?
opt1: Quietly enter and sit down. Apologize to the teacher after class.
opt2: Walk in loudly and announce you're here.
explanation: Enter quietly to avoid disturbing other students. Apologize after class, not during the lesson.
example1: Sorry I'm late. The bus was delayed. May I come in?
example2: I apologize for being late. It won't happen again.

title: School - Forgot Homework
question_text: You forgot your homework. What do you say to the teacher?
opt1: I'm sorry. I forgot my homework. Can I bring it tomorrow?
opt2: I did it but I lost it.
explanation: Be honest and take responsibility. Offer a solution (bring it tomorrow). Lying makes it worse.
example1: My homework is at home. May I email it to you tonight?
example2: I left my homework on the kitchen table. Can I bring it tomorrow?

title: School - Group Project
question_text: A group member isn't doing their share of the work. What do you say to them?
opt1: Can we divide the tasks more clearly? I want to make sure everyone knows their part.
explanation: Address the problem directly but politely. Focus on solutions, not blame.
example1: Which part can you take? We need to finish the slides by Thursday.
example2: Let's check in to see where everyone is. Are you stuck on your part?

title: School - Asking for Help
question_text: You don't understand the math lesson. What do you say to the teacher?
opt1: Could you explain that again? I didn't fully understand.
explanation: Politely ask for clarification. Most teachers are happy to help students who ask.
example1: Could you go over the last part one more time? I want to make sure I get it.
example2: I'm confused about question 5. Can you help me after class?

title: Construction - PPE
question_text: What does PPE stand for?
explanation: PPE = hard hat, safety glasses, high-vis vest, gloves, steel-toe boots, hearing protection.
example1: Before entering the site, check that you have all your PPE. No PPE, no entry.
example2: My PPE includes a hard hat, safety vest, and steel-toed boots.

title: Construction - Fall Protection
question_text: At what height is fall protection required?
explanation: OSHA (US) requires fall protection at 6 feet. Construction is one of the most dangerous jobs because of falls.
example1: Always tie off your harness when working on a roof or scaffold above 6 feet.
example2: I need a harness and lanyard for this roof job.

title: Park - Leave No Trace
question_text: What does 'leave no trace' mean?
opt1: Take all your trash with you. Leave the park as clean as you found it.
opt3: Carve your name into a tree.
explanation: The principle is: don't leave any evidence you were there. Pack it in, pack it out.
example1: We brought a trash bag and carried out all our bottles, wrappers, and apple cores.
example2: Take only pictures. Leave only footprints.

title: Bicycle - Hand Signals
question_text: What does your left arm straight out to the side mean?
explanation: Hand signals tell drivers what you plan to do. Left arm straight out = left turn. Left arm bent up = right turn. Left arm bent down = stop.
example1: I put my left arm out to show I was turning left, but the driver behind me didn't see it.
example2: Always signal before turning or changing lanes.

title: Bicycle - Right of Way
question_text: Who goes first at a 4-way stop when you're on a bike?
example1: Bikes follow the same road rules as cars at stop signs and lights. Don't assume drivers see you.
example2: I arrived at the stop sign at the same time as a car. I waved them to go first to be safe.
example3: It's your turn. You were here first.

title: Cooking - Knife Safety
question_text: What should you never do with a knife?
explanation: A falling knife has no handle. Let it fall. Moving your hand to catch it is how people get severe cuts.
example1: I dropped the chef's knife and just stepped back. It hit the floor. I picked it up carefully.
example2: Don't try to catch a falling knife. Just jump back.

title: Cooking - Smoke Point
question_text: What happens when oil reaches its 'smoke point'?
explanation: The oil doubles in volume.
example2: Every oil has a smoke point. Butter smokes low. Avocado oil smokes very high (520°F). Don't heat oil past its smoke point.
example3: The pan is smoking. You heated the oil too high. Turn down the heat and start over.
example4: Use avocado oil for high-heat cooking like searing steak.

title: Cooking - Food Safety
question_text: What is the 'danger zone' temperature for food?
opt1: Between 40°F and 140°F (4°C - 60°C). Bacteria grows fastest here.
explanation: Don't leave cooked food at room temperature for more than 2 hours. In hot weather, 1 hour. Refrigerate leftovers quickly.
example1: The potato salad sat out for 4 hours. Throw it away. It's not safe to eat.
example2: Put the leftovers in the fridge within 2 hours.

title: Restaurant - Server Mistake
question_text: A server brings you the wrong dish. What do you say?
opt1: Excuse me I ordered the fish not the chicken.
explanation: Politely state the error and what you ordered. Don't be rude. Mistakes happen.
example1: I think there's been a mistake. I ordered the salmon. This looks like halibut.
example2: Excuse me, I asked for no onions. Could I get a new one please?

title: Restaurant - Allergy
question_text: You have a severe nut allergy. What do you tell the server?
opt1: I have a severe nut allergy. Can you tell the chef?
opt3: I'll die if there are nuts.
opt4: Don't put nuts in my food.
explanation: State the allergy clearly and ask them to tell the chef directly. Some kitchens have allergy protocols.
example1: I have a shellfish allergy. Can you make sure my food doesn't touch any shrimp or crab surfaces?
example2: Is this dish prepared in a nut-free area? I have a severe allergy.

title: Restaurant - Tipping
question_text: In the US what is the standard tip at a sit-down restaurant?
explanation: Servers in the US rely on tips because their base wage is low (often $2-5/hour). 18-20% is now common for good service.
example1: The service was excellent. I tipped 25% on a $50 bill = $12.50.
example2: For average service, I tip 18%. For bad service, 10-15% depending.

title: Office - Email Sign-off
sentence: Please let me know if you have any ______.
explanation: Common professional sign-offs: 'Please let me know if you have any questions.'
example1: Best regards, Thomas. Please let me know if you need anything else.
example2: Feel free to reach out if you have any questions.

title: Office - CC in Email
explanation: 'CC' means carbon copy. It sends a copy to someone who needs to see it but isn't the main recipient.
example1: Please CC my manager so she knows the status.
example2: CC the whole team on the update.

title: Office - Voicemail
sentence: Please leave a ______ after the beep.
explanation: The standard voicemail greeting: 'You've reached [name]. I'm unavailable. Please leave a message after the beep.'
example1: You've reached John. I'm away from my desk. Please leave your name and number.
example2: Hi this is Maria. I'm either on the phone or away from my desk. Leave a message and I'll call you back.

title: Factory - Safety Gear
sentence: You must wear ______ protection in this area.
explanation: 'Hearing protection' = earplugs or earmuffs. Factories with loud machines require this.
example1: Hearing protection is mandatory beyond this yellow line.
example2: If it's too loud to talk normally, you need hearing protection.

title: Factory - Inventory
sentence: We need to ______ the parts before the next shift.
explanation: 'Count inventory' = count how many items you have. Done at shift start or end.
example1: Can you count the bolts? We need 500 for tomorrow's order.
example2: Let's do inventory before the truck arrives.

title: School - Raising Hand
sentence: You need to ______ your hand before speaking.
explanation: In most classrooms, you raise your hand to get the teacher's attention. Don't shout out.
example1: Please raise your hand if you know the answer.
example2: Raise your hand if you have a question.

title: School - Final Exam
sentence: The final exam counts for 50 ______ of our grade.
explanation: 'Percent' = per hundred. If the exam is 50%, it's half your total grade.
example1: The final is 40% of your grade, so study hard.
example2: Attendance is 10% of your grade. Homework is 20%.

title: School - Hall Pass
sentence: You need a ______ ______ to leave the classroom.
explanation: A hall pass is a note or lanyard that shows permission to be in the hallway during class.
example1: Can I have a hall pass? I need to go to the nurse.
example2: Don't leave the room without a hall pass.

title: Construction - Hard hat area
explanation: 'Hard hat required' or 'Hard hat area' means you cannot enter without one.
example1: Hard hat required beyond this point. Violators will be removed from site.
example2: Hard hat area. Safety glasses also required.

title: Construction - Tool Belt
explanation: A tool belt or tool pouch holds hammer, tape measure, nail gun, pencil, and other tools.
example1: He reached into his tool belt and pulled out a tape measure.
example2: My tool belt is too heavy. I need to take out some tools.

title: Construction - Blueprint symbol
sentence: The ______ symbol on the blueprint shows where the electrical outlet goes.
explanation: On electrical blueprints, a circle usually represents an outlet or light fixture. Different symbols = different things.
example1: The architect put a circle here. That means a floor outlet, not a wall outlet.
example2: What does this triangle symbol mean on the blueprint?

title: Bicycle - Flat Tire
explanation: 'Flat tire' means no air. You can fix it with a patch kit or replace the inner tube.
example1: I ran over a nail and got a flat tire five miles from home.
example2: My front tire is flat. Do you have a pump?

title: Bicycle - Bell
explanation: Politely ring your bell to let pedestrians know you're passing. Say 'On your left' as you pass.
example1: I rang my bell and said 'Passing on your left.'
example2: Give a little ring so people know you're there.

title: Cooking - Meat Doneness
sentence: Cook the steak to ______ rare for a pink center.
explanation: Medium rare = warm red center. Medium = pink center. Well done = no pink.
example1: I'll have the steak medium rare. If it's well done I'll send it back.
example2: Medium rare is the most popular doneness.

title: Cooking - Oven Preheat
sentence: Always ______ the oven before putting the food in.
explanation: Preheat means turn the oven on early so it reaches the right temperature before you add food.
example1: The cake failed because I didn't preheat the oven. The batter sat too long.
example2: Preheat to 350 degrees. It takes about 10 minutes.

title: Restaurant - Table Turn
sentence: The server wants to ______ the table quickly so the next guests can sit.
explanation: 'Turn a table' = seat a party, serve them, clear the table, and seat the next party. More turns = more money for the restaurant.
example1: We only had 45 minutes for dinner because they needed to flip the table for a 7 PM reservation.
example2: The faster we turn tables, the more money we make.

title: Restaurant - Well done steak
sentence: If you order a steak well ______ it will be cooked through with no pink.
explanation: Well done = no pink, often tougher and drier than medium. Some chefs hate cooking well done steaks.
example1: I know it's a waste, but I like my steak well done with ketchup. Don't judge me.
example2: Well done means no pink in the middle.

title: Restaurant - Corkage fee
sentence: The restaurant charges a ______ fee if you bring your own wine.
explanation: Corkage fee = money charged to open and serve a bottle you brought from home. Usually $10-25. Some restaurants don't allow outside wine.
example1: The corkage fee is $20 per bottle, but the first bottle is free on Mondays.
example2: You can bring your own wine, but there's a $15 corkage fee.
